title: Comments & Readability 📝
description: Write notes in your code that only humans can read.

slides.title: What Are Comments? 💬
slides.content:
# Comments in Python

Comments are **notes you leave in your code** for yourself and other developers. They're like sticky notes on a recipe — they help explain *what* and *why* without changing what happens.

The Python interpreter **completely ignores** comments. They exist only for **human readers**. 👀

slides.title: Single-Line Comments #️⃣
slides.content:
## The Hash Symbol

In Python, you create a comment by starting a line with the **`#`** (hash) symbol. Everything after the `#` on that line is ignored:

```
# This is a comment
print("Hello!")  # This is also a comment
```

The first line is a **full-line comment**. The second line has an **inline comment** — it sits at the end of a line of code.

slides.title: Comments in Action 🎬
slides.content: Notice how comments don't affect the output:
slides.codeSnippet:
# This program greets the user
print("Welcome to Python!")  # Display greeting

# print("This line won't run")
print("Only uncommented lines run!")

quizData.question: Which symbol starts a comment in Python?
quizData.explanation: Python uses the hash symbol (#) for single-line comments. // is used in Java/JS, /* in C, and <!-- in HTML.

slides.title: Multi-Line Comments 📄
slides.content:
## Commenting Multiple Lines

Python doesn't have a dedicated multi-line comment syntax like some languages. Instead, you have two approaches:

**Approach 1 — Multiple hash symbols (recommended):**
```
# This is line one
# This is line two
# This is line three
```

**Approach 2 — Triple-quoted strings:**
```
"""
This is sometimes used
as a multi-line comment.
Python ignores it if it's not assigned.
"""
```

The first approach is the **standard convention**. Triple-quoted strings are technically strings, not comments, but they work similarly when not assigned to a variable.

quizData.question: What is the recommended way to write multi-line comments in Python?

options.text: Using multiple # symbols, one per line

quizData.explanation: The standard convention is to use the # symbol at the start of each line you want to comment out.

slides.title: When to Use Comments 📌
slides.content:
## Best Practices

✅ **DO** comment:
* Complex logic that isn't obvious
* The *why* behind a decision
* Important warnings or TODOs

❌ **DON'T** comment:
* Things that are already obvious
* Every single line of code

**Bad:** `x = 5  # Set x to 5` (we can see that!)

**Good:** `x = 5  # Default retry limit per docs` (now we know WHY!)

slides.title: Commenting Out Code 🚫
slides.content:
## A Debugging Superpower!

One of the most useful tricks is **commenting out code** — temporarily disabling lines without deleting them:

```
print("Step 1")
# print("Step 2")  <- temporarily disabled
print("Step 3")
```

This is incredibly useful when **debugging** (finding and fixing errors). You can disable code line by line to isolate which line is causing a problem! 🔍

quizData.question: What does 'commenting out' code mean?

options.text: Adding a # to temporarily disable the code
Writing a description of what the code does

quizData.explanation: Commenting out code means adding a # at the start of a line to temporarily prevent it from running, without actually deleting it.
